shortDescription.text: Passwords stored in plaintext without hashing

fullDescription.text: The users table schema defines password as TEXT NOT NULL with no hashing or key derivation applied before storage. The authenticate_user method compares passwords directly as plaintext strings. Any database compromise exposes all user credentials immediately.

shortDescription.text: Hardcoded default credentials seeded into database

fullDescription.text: The init_database method inserts hardcoded admin/admin123 and guest/guest credentials with known API keys. These credentials are trivially guessable and ship with the application, granting immediate admin access to any attacker.

shortDescription.text: API keys hardcoded in source code and stored in plaintext

fullDescription.text: API keys sk-1234567890abcdef and pk-0987654321fedcba are hardcoded in source and stored as plaintext in the database. The authenticate_user method also returns the api_key field to the caller, exposing it on every successful login.

shortDescription.text: Arbitrary code execution via pickle.loads on user-controlled input

fullDescription.text: update_user_preferences calls pickle.loads on the preferences string parameter. Pickle deserialization of untrusted data allows arbitrary code execution by crafting a malicious pickle payload containing __reduce__ instructions.

shortDescription.text: Sensitive fields (session_token, api_key, email) stored without encryption

fullDescription.text: The users table stores api_key, session_token, and email as plain TEXT columns with no application-level encryption. A database compromise or SQL injection gives direct access to all secrets.

shortDescription.text: SQL injection in authentication query via f-string interpolation

fullDescription.text: authenticate_user constructs the SQL query using f-string formatting with unsanitized username and password inputs, enabling authentication bypass and data extraction via SQL injection.

shortDescription.text: SQL queries containing credentials logged at DEBUG level

fullDescription.text: The authenticate_user method logs the full SQL query including plaintext username and password at DEBUG level. This exposes credentials in log files, stdout, and any log aggregation systems.

shortDescription.text: API key returned in authentication response dictionary

fullDescription.text: The authenticate_user method includes the user's api_key in the returned dictionary on successful login. This unnecessarily exposes the API key to any code path that handles the auth result, increasing the attack surface for key leakage.

message.text: The users table stores passwords as plaintext TEXT. No hashing (bcrypt, argon2, scrypt) is applied before INSERT or compared during authentication. A single SQL injection or DB file access exposes every password.
Hardcoded credentials admin/admin123 (role=admin) and guest/guest (role=user) are inserted on every database initialization. These are well-known defaults that grant immediate privileged access.

API keys sk-1234567890abcdef and pk-0987654321fedcba are hardcoded in source code and stored as plaintext in the api_key column. Keys are recoverable from source control history even if later removed.

pickle.loads() is called on user-supplied preferences string at line 143. An attacker can craft a pickle payload that executes arbitrary system commands via __reduce__, achieving full remote code execution.

Sensitive columns api_key, session_token, and email are stored as plain TEXT with no application-level encryption (AES-GCM, etc.). Combined with SQL injection vulnerabilities in this file, all secrets are directly extractable.

authenticate_user at line 92 builds a SQL query via f-string with raw username and password. Input like username=' OR 1=1-- bypasses authentication entirely. Parameterized queries must be used instead.
Line 93 logs the full SQL query at DEBUG level, which contains the plaintext username and password. Credentials will appear in any log sink (files, stdout, centralized logging).
authenticate_user returns the api_key field in its result dictionary at line 105. This leaks the key to all downstream consumers of the auth response, violating least-privilege for secret access.
